utterances: And you can hear more of the interview with Bill Friedkin and see the French Connection chase scene at our Web site, NPR.org.
Four months have past since Iraqis cast ballots for the country's first full-term post-Saddam parliament.
But lawmakers have yet to form a coalition government.
Today the acting speaker postponed Monday's planned session of Parliament to give parties more time to agree on key government posts.
Just before the December elections, we spoke with four Washington, D.C.
area residents of Iraqi descent who were voting.
Today we check back in with them.
Bala Wahab(ph) is a Fulbright scholar studying at American University.
Babylonia Marcus(ph) works for an international development company.
And Yassir Shalal(ph) is a businessman.
They joined me in the studio.
Ali al Atar(ph), a physician, was on the phone.
I asked them about the political paralysis four months after the vote.
Yassir Shalal called the stalemate unfortunate.
Everybody had hopes in the most recent election that this process, this log jam, would break open and in fact there would be a mandate to govern.
But these things take time.
The Iraqis have not been given enough time.
The process has been driven by Washington politics, not Iraqi needs.
You know, we saw the voting fall much along sectarian lines and now there is growing sectarian strife.
Just about every day there are new reports.
Let's get you in on the conversation from the telephone.
Dr. al Atar, you are just back from Baghdad.
Do you think Iraq is ready for democracy?
There are thousands of gadgets to recharge phones but nothing to recharge relationships.
Apparently, the most important relationship you have is with yourself.
I don't need anybody as long as I have my own drone.
What does this say?
Oh, absolutely.
Better than I could, that's for sure.
We want them to know that they have to continue their studies throughout their lives, and everything is going to be changing rapidly.
No longer will people be working for the same company for 30 years and 35 years as so many of their parents or grandparents did, and they have to be prepared to make changes.
So they have to be able to use a computer not only for games and for fun, but for learning.
And we want them to learn how to take a course on the computer.
To learn some defined objective...
Right.
...in some way that is laid out by someone else.
Yes, like with a course in a school.
And we're saying it can be either a credit or a non-credit course.
And it might even be a half-a-semester course, but just to get the feel for actually studying and learning something new on the computer rather than just sort of hit-and-miss Internet surfing.
Well, would it be Michigan's goal that one actually apply this to something that you really, really need to know.
Say, would you take American history or English or algebra or something online?
You could.
What we're think--a lot of what we were thinking--we included in our requirements two credits of world languages, and we thought we don't have enough teachers necessarily to teach world language.
We might not have enough teachers to teach Chinese or Japanese, something like that, and that would be a good thing to teach on the Internet.
The state Legislature still has to really pass this.
Right.
The state Legislature really has to endorse what we did and...
And the governor and all that.
But still, Michigan will be the first state in the country to have this requirement if this all goes through.
We think so.
We're not aware of other states doing this.
Ms.

Straus, did you and the Board of Education consider at all, or did anyone raise the argument, `Well, there will be some families where kids can't afford computers,' or do you think that things are now in such a state that really everyone does have a computer or access?
No, we know that some students--they don't have computers in their homes...
Yeah.
...but we do have--in the public libraries, there are computers, and there are libraries in the school which they could use.
So we think that there will be computers available to everybody, even if they don't have it at home.
I wonder if you think Michigan is particularly forward-thinking about this because of your experience with the automobile industry.
So many people think, `Well, Michigan's economy is perennially in trouble 'cause it's tied to heavy manufacturing, and that's not what the future economy's going to be in this country.'
Well, that's right.
That's why we wanted to have this state requirement for graduation and change the way people do business.
We wanted to make a change.
We know that we have to make a change.
Things are not going to be the way they were, especially in Michigan.
And so we were looking to the future and thinking, `Well, this is a way to--this might be the way to go.'
Kathleen Straus is president of the Michigan State Board of Education, which this week passed a resolution saying that future high school graduates will have to take at least one course online.
Ms.
Straus, thank you for speaking with us on DAY TO DAY.
Thank you.
And DAY TO DAY continues.
Stay with us.
I'm Alex Chadwick.
Joined now by David Leonhardt, economics columnist for the New York Times.
Morning, David.
Good morning, Scott.
How are you?
Fine, thank you.
And so are a lot of people feeling better, I gather.
We just heard that piece about the markets.
And the president's top economics adviser, Larry Summers, even suggested yesterday stocks may be the sale of the century.
Is this what a wise man once called irrational exuberance?
Well, there is some economic rationale to what Summers said yesterday.
My favorite measure of the stock market is this long-term price-earnings ratio, which removes a lot of the fictional aspect of price-earnings ratio; it doesn't include forecasts.
It's remarkably consistent over history.
And it's - the average over the century has been about 16, and we're now at about 13.
And that means we're below average; stocks appear to be undervalued.
Typically, when stocks have been at about 13, they've been pretty well over the next decade or two.
But that's - a decade or two is a long time.
And during the other two great bear markets of the last century, in the 1930s and early 1980s, that ratio fell all the way to six or seven.
So while it's true that stocks are cheap, if we follow the same path of other huge recessions and bear markets, they still have a long way to fall before they come roaring back.
As we noted, financial leaders already in England laying the groundwork for next month's G20 meeting in London.
We'll get to Europe in a second, but let's begin with China's concern.
The Chinese premier has really said they're worried about China's holdings in U.S.
Treasury bonds.
Yeah.
And there's some politics here and some economics.
The politics, as you mentioned, is this G20 meeting.
The U.S.
has not exactly been showering attention on China.
In fact, there was a little incident between the U.S.
and Chinese navies this week.
There was the thing a few weeks ago in which Tim Geithner said that the Chinese are manipulating their currency.
...And so he named you Prawnche?
And so I think was China reminding the U.S.
in the run-up to the G20 that not only are they significant but arguably, China is the most significant county in the world besides the U.S.
And the reason for that is the economics.
China has lent the U.S.
an enormous amount of money over the last decade.
It's fueled our consumption boom.
It's allowed us to buy all those toys and shoes and things from China.
And because of the recession, we've now had to go even deeper in debt.
And so what China was saying here was, look, you need a plan to repay your debt because if you don't have one, we're worried about the money we lent you.
We're effectively worried about the value of our investment in you.
And, you know, when you see the story this morning, you don't think it has anything to do with health care, but I actually think this is directly related to health care.
By far, our biggest long-term budget problem is health care.
So in a funny way, you could read that story as the Chinese telling us to get serious about bringing down our health-care costs.
What are the European countries, as a generalization, hoping to get at the G20 talks?
There's been this tension in the run-up in the meeting between the U.S.
and Europe.
It doesn't seem to be quite as stark as the tension between the U.S.
and the Chinese.
But the Europeans want to focus more on the regulation of the financial system and of capitalism after the crisis is over.
And the U.S.
wants to focus more on stimulating the economy.
And I would say that in the short term, the U.S.
really is more right than Europe on this, although in the long term, Europe is right that we in the U.S., in particular, didn't do a good enough job of regulating our economy.
And so what the U.S.
wants to see is more of a commitment from Europe, the kind of commitment that we have made in this country and that China has made, quite frankly, to stimulate the economy.
And we and China probably haven't even gone far enough.
The Europeans and particularly, the Germans - it would be really quite nice to see them taking the downturn more seriously and doing more to stimulate their economies.
And finally, Bernie Madoff in the clink this weekend after having admitted his guilt in his role in the Ponzi scheme that left so many people devastated.
But there are still a lot of questions, aren't there, about if anyone else was involved, and is there any money to be recovered?
Yeah, I think there are two big questions.
One is the one you just mentioned - is anyone else involved?
It just strains credibility that no one else was.
It's hard to imagine Bernie Madoff staying up at night typing up these fake investment returns.
And the second is, what lesson do we take?
I mean, I said the Europeans are wrong on stimulus, but they're right on regulation.
We could've caught Bernie Madoff, and we didn't.
And so what you really have to hope going forward is that we have people in Washington who take claims of fraud more seriously.
There were people who came forward and accused Bernie Madoff of fraud, and they were ignored.
And let's hope that doesn't happen again.
David Leonhardt, economics columnist for the New York Times, thanks so much.
Thanks for having me.
